title: St. Andronicus, St. Athanasius of Alexandria, St. Konon the Gardener & The 40 Martyrs of Sebaste
narrative:
title": "St. Andronicus, St. Athanasius of Alexandria, St. Konon the Gardener & The 40 Martyrs of Sebaste

narrative": "title\": \"St. Andronicus, St. Athanasius of Alexandria, St. Konon the Gardener & The 40 Martyrs of Sebaste

narrative\": \"St. Andronicus the Apostle, St. Athanasius the Great of Alexandria, St. Konon the Gardener, and the Forty Martyrs of Sebaste

On the ninth of Megabit, the holy Church celebrates the monthly commemoration of the Forty Martyrs of Sebaste — those soldiers of Christ who stood through a night of ice and wind on the frozen lake rather than deny their Lord — alongside three witnesses whose lives resonate deeply with this month's Lenten character. The Forty Martyrs return this month in the register of Lenten combat, their overnight ordeal on the lake becoming an image of the Great Fast itself: a prolonged exposure to cold, hunger, and the proximity of a warm bath that offers immediate relief if only one condition is met. The bath that one soldier ran to, and into which Lent does not permit the faithful to enter, is the comfort of the world — warm, immediate, reliably available, and bought at the price of the confession. The Forty who stayed on the ice are the Lenten assembly.

Saint Andronicus is one of the seventy apostles, greeted by Paul in the Letter to the Romans as a kinsman who had been in Christ before Paul himself — which makes him among the earliest identifiable Christians in the New Testament outside the Twelve. He and his companion Junia (whom Paul calls notable among the apostles) are honoured together in the Eastern tradition as missionaries who carried the Gospel to the regions of Pannonia and Achaia. The Ethiopian Church venerates Andronicus as a representative of the apostolic generation beyond the Twelve — the wider circle of those who were in Christ from the beginning and whose witness sustained the communities between the visits of the travelling apostles.

Saint Athanasius the Great — Athanasius contra mundum, the theologian who withstood the entire weight of imperial power and ecclesiastical politics for the sake of the single Greek word homoousios — is the towering figure of this day's assembly. His five exiles and five returns, spread across more than four decades of episcopal ministry, constitute one of the most dramatic stories of institutional combat in the history of Christianity, and his theological achievement — the preservation and elaboration of the Nicene faith in a period when every emperor but one was pressing for compromise — shaped the doctrinal foundation on which all subsequent Christology rests. The Ethiopian Church, which confesses the Nicene faith in its fullness, honours Athanasius as the father of that confession in its historical form.

Saint Konon the Gardener is this day's figure of quiet, earthy holiness: a Christian of Isauria who tended a garden near his village and who, when the persecution came, was arrested not for any public act of evangelism or institutional leadership but simply for being a Christian, for being what he was in the place where he worked. His interrogators asked him whether he was of the family of Christ — perhaps aware of his claim to Nazarene lineage — and he replied with complete simplicity that he was. He was tortured and forced to run before horses until he fell, dying in the road. His gardener's hands and his gardener's simplicity are the tradition's image of the holiness that needs no public stage, that grows like a plant in its own soil, and that is recognised as fully martyrological by the same authority that crowns the generals and the bishops. Amen.
